VanIQ Easy Lift

Motorless e-bike door lift · Fits any high-roof van · Motor optional
Canonical product document · Internal · Prototype v0.1 · Pivoting parallelogram cradle lift, T-slot construction

Easy Lift loads your e-bike for you. Lift only the front wheel into the cradle; the gas struts do the rest. The rear wheel stays on the ground until the bike is already rising, and two positive pins lock the bike and the lift for the road.

Executive Review

Concept

The VanIQ Easy Lift is significantly more than another bike rack. It addresses a genuine problem created by the rapid growth of heavy e-bikes (up to 80 lb): owners can no longer easily lift their bikes into vans or onto traditional racks. The core value is not the winch, gas struts, or frame construction — it is the loading geometry.

The lift lets the user keep the rear wheel on the ground, lift only the front wheel into a fixed cradle, positively lock the wheel, and then raise the bike into a secure transport position. This substantially reduces physical effort while maintaining a secure mechanical connection throughout the lift.

Major strengths

Innovative loading method. The parallelogram linkage keeps cradle orientation constant through the lift, so the wheel stays captured as the bike goes from ground loading to transport. This geometry is the primary innovation and the greatest differentiator.

Safety-first design. The thru-bolt wheel lock serves four independent purposes: positive wheel retention during lifting, transport safety lock, theft deterrent (with a lock fitted), and a frame-safe attachment in the proven 1UP wheel-retention philosophy. This is a primary marketing message, not just a component.

Modular actuation. The lift mechanism is independent of the actuator — the same linkage runs on gas struts, manual rope assist, or an electric DC lift. Multiple configurations, one core mechanism.

Prototype methodology. T-slot extrusion is the right choice for the prototype: easy adjustment, rapid modification, simple fabrication, fast geometry validation. Production transitions to welded or formed aluminum after testing.

Primary business opportunity

The opportunity is larger than selling a bike lift. The real product is a rear-door mounting platform that supports multiple accessories: e-bike lift, cargo basket, jerry-can carrier, recovery-board mount, tire carrier, ski rack, utility platform. This drives recurring sales while customers keep the original mounting system.

Validation priorities

Before commercialization, testing should validate customer confidence over theoretical calculation. Priorities: long-term hinge durability, door deflection measurement, dynamic road testing, repeated loading cycles, ease-of-use testing with real users, and installation-time reduction. The structural calculations already show healthy margins; real-world durability testing will be more persuasive than additional analysis.

Product development strategy

  • Phase 1 — Build and validate the adjustable prototype.
  • Phase 2 — Optimize geometry and reduce part count.
  • Phase 3 — Replace T-slot with production aluminum tubing or formed members.
  • Phase 4 — Design installation to ~2 hours using preassembled hinge plates, door-mount assemblies, and lift modules.

Customer Benefits

Easy Lift loads your e-bike for you. One wheel up, and it’s on.

  • One-hand loading — Lift only the front wheel into the cradle. No dead-lifting heavy bikes onto a rack.
  • Fits most bikes & e-bikes — Up to 80 lb. A front-wheel thru-bolt holds the bike secure during lift and on the road.
  • Fits any high-roof van — Mounts to the rear of the van door; the lift is platform-agnostic. Works with the VanIQ Transit mount and other van platforms like Sherpa.
  • Power — Smooth gas-strut assist, or optional DC power lift with wireless remote (easy same-rack add-on).
  • Ultra-light & easy install — Rack weighs under 35 lb (rack only). Installs in under 2 hours with no drilling. One-person job.
  • Future-proof — One mounting frame supports multiple accessories, so you only buy what you need today.

Design Summary validated config

ItemSpecNotes
Bike targetup to 80 lb, 29″ wheel, 50.5″ wheelbaseXL frame
Anchor height40″ off groundpivot centerline
Anchor standoff~0.5″ (inside door-mount frame)within frame’s 2″ depth
Arm length40″ pivot-to-pivotboth links equal
Arm spacing10″ verticalsets parallelogram
Load arm angle-46° (shallow, down)rear wheel stays at 0″
Stow arm angle90° (vertical)bike stands up
Load cradle base11.7″ off groundowner lifts front axle to 26.2″
Stow cradle base80.4″ off groundfront wheel top 109″, roof +3″
Rear wheel at stow29.5″ off groundclear for transport
Sweep136° load-to-stow—
Rope tension~35 lb at 2:1powered option: huge margin

Load sequence: bike stands on ground (rear wheel at 0″). Owner lifts the front wheel into the lowered cradle and locks the thru-bolt. The arms sweep up (struts or winch); the bike rotates onto and then off its rear wheel to vertical stow. The transport pin then locks the lift so the rope/struts are unloaded.

Bike Load Point (Center of Gravity)

Every joint force is driven by where the bike’s weight acts — its center of gravity — not where the wheels touch. For an e-bike the CG sits low and near the motor/battery. This document assumes a mid-drive layout: CG ~46% of the wheelbase behind the front axle (~23″ back), ~12.5″ off the ground when level. Confirm against the actual bike before final analysis — a rear-hub motor with a rack battery shifts the CG rearward.

At load, the cradle carries the front-axle share (~38 lb) and the ground carries the rest (~32 lb). As the arms lift and the bike rotates vertical, the rear wheel leaves the ground and the cradle, arms, and pivots carry the full bike weight through the CG. The CG’s offset from the cradle sets the moment the structure must hold.

Section 1 — Cradle

Two right-triangle plates form a wheel pocket. The wheel sits in the V between the two long diagonal legs. A thru-bolt passes through both diagonal legs, over the wheel, clamping it into the cradle — the proven 1UP-style lock. The cradle carries two pivot bosses (upper and lower) for the parallelogram links.

PartQtySpec
Triangle plates A & B21/4″ 6061, ~12×10″ right triangle
Plate spacers2–35″ tube — sets tire pocket
Pivot bushings4bronze flanged, 1/2″ ID
Thru-bolt (wheel lock)11/2″×7″ + clamp handle
Tire pad1rubber/UHMW on base (optional)

Section 2 — Arm Assembly

A parallelogram linkage: an upper and a lower link of equal length on each side of the wheel — four links total. Because the links stay parallel through the sweep, the cradle holds a constant angle and the wheel never falls out. The rope/webbing attaches to the upper links (2:1 reeve).

PartQtySpec
Links (arms)440″ 1020 T-slot — 2 upper + 2 lower
Anchor pivot bolts41/2″ shoulder, double shear
Cradle pivot bolts41/2″ shoulder, double shear
Bushings81/2″ ID bronze, one per pivot

Section 3 — Anchor Mount

The anchor carries the four arm pivots and is built into the door-mount frame, using its 2″ depth — no separate standoff. Because the 40″ arms are far longer than the bumper sticks out (~8″), the cradle still swings past the bumper to reach the grounded front wheel at load. Pivot bolts run in double shear through gusseted brackets.

PartQtySpec
Anchor uprights21530, integral to door frame
Gusset plates41/4″ 6061 triangle gussets
Pivot bolts41/2″ shoulder, double shear
Pivot bushings41/2″ ID bronze, press fit

Section 4 — Door Mount

A T-slot frame (14″ wide × 52″ tall) bolts to the rear door at three points using flat plates that connect the frame to the hinge kit. Two plates land on the upper and lower hinge points; the third ties to an interior point. The anchor attaches on horizontal 1020 rails between the two vertical rails. The door easily handles the ~70 lb bike load (rated ~200 lb).

PartQtySpec
Vertical rails252″ 1530 T-slot
Horizontal rails414″ 1020 (frame + anchor rails)
Hinge-kit flat plates31/4″ 6061, drilled to hinge kit
Diagonal brace1back-plane or corner gussets (anti-rack)

Load Path & Structural Check

The load path runs from the bike’s CG through the cradle, arms, anchor, and door-mount frame into the three hinge-kit points. The parallelogram converts what would be bending in the arms into axial tension/compression, which is why light 1020 extrusion is adequate. Every joint closes with margin.

JointLoadMargin
Cradle thru-bolt~35 lb double shearSF >50×
Cradle plates~350 in-lb bendingSF ~4× (governing)
Arm links (axial)~56 lb axialSF ~18×
Anchor pivot bolts~91 lb double shearSF >30×
Door 3-point~35 lb peak pull-outSF ~5.7× (200 lb door)

Governing item: the cradle plates at ~4×. Stepping them to 3/8″ adds margin for ~$20. Door capacity is never the limit — peak demand on any single mount point is ~35 lb.

Actuator — Motor OR Manual

The lift mechanism (cradle, arms, anchor, door mount) is identical whether powered or manual. Only the actuator at the rope-anchor point changes, so the motor is optional — build either, or build the frame to accept both.

Optional DC power lift

The powered version uses a 12V DC winch drawing from the van’s house/EcoFlow system through a fused switch, ~3 A peak. It is a self-contained sealed unit with its own gear-train brake and a wireless remote — no separate controller or limit switches to wire. The frame, geometry, and load path are unchanged whether a winch or gas struts sit at the actuator point, so a builder can start manual and add the motor later.

Manual (gas struts + rope with friction brake)

No wiring, no battery draw. Two gas struts do most of the work; the operator initiates the rise and the rope only eases the bike through the mid-sweep hump and controls lowering. A rope clutch / friction brake near the apex pulley lets the user lower the bike slowly and pause at any point hands-free — winch-like control at a fraction of the cost.

How the system works together

  • Gas struts provide most of the required lift force.
  • Operator effort peaks at ~14 lb through the 2:1 rope at the hump.
  • Friction brake allows smooth, controlled lowering — ease the rope through the brake.
  • Cam cleat locks the rope at any position for loading or parking.

Webbing-to-rope: flat 2″ nylon webbing attaches softly at the upper arm/cradle, transitions via thimble splice to 3/8″ marine double-braid rope for the section that runs over the pulley and through the brake. Keep the splice clear of the sheave.

Strut Geometry — Over-Center, Zero Lift at Load

The struts use an over-center mount that produces zero lift force at the load position, then engages as the arm rises. This satisfies a hard requirement: when the arms are extended down to receive the bike, the struts must not push up against the owner.

A gas strut always pushes along its axis — you cannot switch it off. But mounted so its axis passes through the arm pivot at load, its moment arm is zero and it produces no lift torque. As the arm rises, the axis swings off the pivot, the moment arm opens (0″ → 1.4″ → 3.1″ → 4.7″), and lift ramps in. Near vertical the moment arm closes again, so the strut eases off — no throw at the top.

Strut spec (per side)

  • 24″ extended, 150 lb (668 N), ~9.7″ stroke
  • Lower end anchored to door frame 18″ from arm pivot
  • Upper end attached to lower arm 6″ from pivot
Build note: make the door-anchor mount adjustable (slot or a few holes) so you can dial the dead-center point on the bench until lift force reads ~0 at load.

Lowering the Bike & Transport Lock

Getting the bike down safely, and keeping it from descending on its own during transport, is the most safety-critical part of operation. The over-center struts cushion the descent (they push hardest mid-descent, so the bike cannot free-fall), and a positive lock pin holds the bike during transport — independent of the struts.

Lowering sequence (safe order)

  1. Stand beside the bike and take the rope/webbing in hand.
  2. Pull the rope to take tension — this unloads the lock pin.
  3. Remove the lock pin (only now, with the rope holding the weight).
  4. Ease the rope out hand-over-hand; the struts cushion the descent.
  5. The bike rotates down, rear wheel touches ground, front settles to load.
  6. Lift the front wheel out of the cradle.
Safety rule: the pin comes out only AFTER the rope has tension, and the rope goes slack only AFTER the pin is back in. The pin and rope hand the load to each other — the bike is never held by nothing.

Transport lock

At vertical stow a hole in the lower arm aligns with a hole in a lock bracket fixed to the door frame. A 1/2″ clevis pin drops through both, captured by a lynch/R-clip on a lanyard. This pin is a positive mechanical stop and carries the bike on the road. Never drive on strut force alone: struts slowly lose pressure over years and can compress momentarily on a bump. Make the bracket position adjustable so the pin seats clean at true vertical.

Why the Gas-Strut Design Works

A gas strut always pushes — you cannot turn it off. The classic problem is that it pushes the arms up even when the cradle is lowered for loading, fighting the user. The solution is to mount the strut so its line of force passes exactly through the arm pivot at the load position: zero moment arm, zero torque. As soon as the arm rotates a few degrees, the strut moves off-center and begins helping — natural, progressive assistance exactly where it is needed and none where it is not. It is the mechanical equivalent of a variable-force spring without any electronics.

Is 136° range of motion too much?

No — it is appropriate. Load angle -46° (shallow, rear wheel on the ground) to stow +90° (vertical) is a 136° sweep. Benefits:

  • Front wheel starts low (11.7″ off ground) so almost anyone can load the bike.
  • Excellent rear-wheel ground clearance during rotation (never drags).
  • Stowed bike is compact and high enough to clear the bumper and rear-door hardware.
  • The parallelogram keeps cradle orientation constant, so the wheel stays captured through the entire sweep.

Dimensioned Side Elevation

Full assembly to scale, showing the load position (arms shallow at -46°, bike grounded, cradle base 11.7″) and the stow position (arms vertical, front wheel top 109″, rear wheel 29.5″ off ground), against the ground, bumper (28″), and roof (110″) references.

Design Refinements & v0.2 Path

Cradle v2 — tube instead of plates v0.2

The plate cradle is overbuilt for this short-span job. A v2 cradle in 1–1.25″ square aluminum tube with corner blocks cuts cradle weight roughly in half, lowers cost, and lets the V-angle be tuned for tire width. The thru-bolt lock carries over unchanged. Build plates for v0.1 to validate geometry fast, then switch to tube for v0.2.

Friction brake on the manual lower recommended

A rope clutch / line brake on the marine-rope section, ahead of the cam cleat, lets the user control lowering speed and self-arrests if grip slips. Adds redundancy to the over-center struts already cushioning the descent.

Door-frame diagonal brace v0.1

The 14×52″ rectangle racks toward a parallelogram unless triangulated. A diagonal (back-plane, or short corner gussets) carries only ~27 lb but prevents the loosening/play that road vibration causes over time. Cheap insurance on the one part under constant vibration with a cantilevered load.

Estimated Cost

Materials only (hinge kit purchased separately). T-slot at surplus $0.50/ft; budget the retail column for new 1530 ($3–6/ft). Cost is nearly a wash between powered and manual.

ItemCostNotes
T-slot extrusion$19 (surplus) / ~$200 (retail)38 ft
Aluminum plate$110cradle, hinge plates, gussets
Pivot + fastener hardware$236shoulder bolts, bushings, tee nuts
Door/hinge mount hardware$90Grade-8 bolts, interior pt, sealing
Actuator (powered)$200DC winch + rope
Actuator (manual)$17024″ struts, pulley, webbing/rope, brake, cleat

Powered: ~$690 (surplus) to ~$870 (retail). Manual: ~$640 (surplus) to ~$820 (retail). Hinge kit (~$218, owned) not included.

If built as a product (wholesale)

At volume, parts drop ~40–45% (~$480–520/unit), but a viable product would still retail $700–900 once labor, tooling, packaging, warranty, and margin are added. The larger lever is design-for-manufacture, not the raw parts discount.

Revision History

VersionNotes
v0.1Prototype — initial build & bench-test design. Parallelogram cradle, 40″ anchor, 40″ arm, 24″/150 lb over-center struts, friction brake, door diagonal brace, motor optional.
Prototype status: v0.1 is a working design for the first physical build and bench testing — not a production release. After building: verify door-structure mount points, dial in the strut dead-center anchor, confirm the bike CG against the actual bike, and field-check all dimensions before cutting final material.
